skill.name: firecrawl-build-onboarding
skill.description: Guide an AI coding agent through Firecrawl API key setup, SDK installation, and first integration steps for an application project.
skill.summary: Set up Firecrawl credentials, SDKs, and project configuration for app integrations.
skill.author: firecrawl

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns, but review shows these are documented setup steps for Firecrawl onboarding rather than hidden executable code. The skill still has medium risk because it asks agents to run package-manager commands, complete a network auth flow, and store API keys in .env files.

medium_findings.title: API Key Storage in Environment Files
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to place Firecrawl API keys in .env files. This is legitimate setup guidance, but mishandling can leak credentials through source control, logs, or shared workspaces.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files directly document FIRECRAWL_API_KEY placement in .env. The usage is expected, but API keys are sensitive and require careful handling.

medium_findings.title: External Installer and Package Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends npx, npm, pip, openssl, and shell commands. These commands support onboarding, but package-manager execution can run third-party code or modify the local project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clear and relevant to Firecrawl setup. Risk remains because external packages and shell commands may affect the user environment.

medium_findings.title: Network Authentication Flow Handles Sensitive Values
medium_findings.description: The documented auth flow sends session and verifier values to Firecrawl endpoints and returns an API key. This is expected, but it depends on the remote service handling tokens securely.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network flow is explicit and targets Firecrawl domains, matching the skill purpose. It still processes authentication material and generated API keys.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misread as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts are from fenced markdown examples, not hidden Ruby or shell execution by the skill itself. The examples are visible setup instructions for the user or agent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text appears inside markdown documentation blocks. There is no executable script file or hidden runtime wrapper in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Misread as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The ../ references point to sibling skill documentation pages. I did not find evidence of filesystem traversal, file reads, or arbitrary path access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only traversal-like strings are markdown links to nearby skill files. They are not used by code to access arbitrary paths.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Alerts Are PKCE Hashing Examples
low_findings.description: The openssl commands generate random session values and a SHA-256 code challenge for browser authorization. I did not find weak algorithms such as MD5 or SHA-1 in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed command uses sha256 for a code challenge and random values for an auth flow. The static weak-crypto classification is not supported by the visible context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Latest-Version CLI Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The npx command executes the latest Firecrawl CLI package and may change project files or browser state. Users should review package provenance before running it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact npx command is present and uses @latest. It is legitimate for onboarding but has supply-chain and local modification implications.

dangerous_patterns.title: Appending Secrets to .env
dangerous_patterns.description: The auth reference shows appending a Firecrawl API key to .env. This can duplicate secrets or place credentials in files that are accidentally committed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command explicitly writes FIRECRAWL_API_KEY to .env. The credential value is a placeholder, but real usage would involve a secret.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Auth Status Polling
dangerous_patterns.description: The auth reference posts session credentials to a Firecrawl status endpoint. This is expected for CLI onboarding but should be limited to the documented Firecrawl service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and request body are visible in the documentation. The risk is contextual because it is part of the intended authentication flow.

content.value_statement: Developers need a repeatable way to add Firecrawl authentication and SDK dependencies to an app. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through API key setup, SDK selection, and first integration checks.
actual_capabilities: Guides Firecrawl API key setup for hosted and self-hosted projects.
Documents a browser authorization flow for users without an existing key.
Shows JavaScript, TypeScript, and Python SDK installation commands.
Explains where to place FIRECRAWL_API_KEY and FIRECRAWL_API_URL.
Points agents to source-of-truth documentation for supported languages.
Frames next steps for fresh projects and existing applications.
limitations: It does not validate that a real Firecrawl request succeeds by itself.
It assumes the user can authorize access through Firecrawl browser auth.
It provides setup guidance, not endpoint-specific scraping logic.
It requires careful handling of API keys and .env files.

use_cases.title: Add Firecrawl to a New App
use_cases.description: Choose the initial SDK path, add the key, and prepare a first smoke test.

use_cases.title: Standardize Agent Onboarding
use_cases.description: Give an AI coding agent a consistent checklist for Firecrawl credentials and setup.

use_cases.title: Configure Self-Hosted Firecrawl
use_cases.description: Document both the API key and custom Firecrawl API URL for deployment environments.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up My Project
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an existing repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Firecrawl build onboarding skill to inspect this project and tell me the safest way to add Firecrawl credentials and SDK support.

prompt_templates.title: Add Credentials Safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Environment variable setup with secret handling.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add FIRECRAWL_API_KEY guidance for this project. Check existing .env patterns and avoid exposing the key in source files.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the SDK Path
prompt_templates.scenario: Select an integration route for a specific stack.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the onboarding references to choose the right Firecrawl SDK or REST path for this codebase, then explain the required install and config steps.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Production Onboarding
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a Firecrawl setup plan for development, preview, and production, including secret storage, self-hosted URL support, and smoke test criteria.

output_examples.input: I have a Node app and need Firecrawl added.
output_examples.output: A concise setup plan covering API key storage, JavaScript SDK installation, where to add configuration, and a smoke test checklist.

output_examples.input: We use a self-hosted Firecrawl endpoint.
output_examples.output: Guidance to set both the API key and custom API URL, plus notes for matching development and production environments.

output_examples.input: I do not have a Firecrawl API key yet.
output_examples.output: Instructions for the browser authorization flow, how the key is received, and how to store it without hardcoding secrets.

best_practices: Confirm .env files are ignored before writing or documenting API keys.
Use a platform secret manager for shared, preview, and production environments.
Read the language-specific Firecrawl documentation before adding integration code.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode FIRECRAWL_API_KEY in application source files.
Do not run installer commands without reviewing package provenance and project impact.
Do not treat setup as complete until a real Firecrawl smoke test succeeds.

faq.question: Does this skill make Firecrawl API calls?
faq.answer: No. It provides setup and onboarding guidance. The user or agent must run any chosen commands or tests.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it support self-hosted Firecrawl?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents FIRECRAWL_API_URL for self-hosted deployments alongside FIRECRAWL_API_KEY.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: It handles API keys, network auth, and package-manager commands. These are legitimate but need careful review.

faq.question: Can it choose between SDK and REST usage?
faq.answer: Yes. It tells agents to inspect the project and use the narrowest integration path that fits the stack.

faq.question: Does it replace Firecrawl endpoint-specific skills?
faq.answer: No. It handles onboarding, then points agents to endpoint-specific skills for implementation details.
